metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify that story e4df / change 660 has been merged and `signing.sign_opcert_manifest()` + `opcert.opcert_from_record()` are importable before scoping this story as expand-only; if not, either add their implementation to this story's scope or add an explicit hard dependency gate.
findings.finding: The plan's premise that the storage keystone (e4df, change 660) 'already gives `signing.sign_opcert_manifest()` an envelope-bearing SIGNATURE event and `opcert.opcert_from_record()` read-back' is unverifiable: neither `sign_opcert_manifest` nor `opcert_from_record` nor any `rebar.opcert` module exists anywhere in the repo.
findings.location: Why section: 'The storage keystone (e4df, change 660) already gives `signing.sign_opcert_manifest()` an envelope-bearing `SIGNATURE` event and `opcert.opcert_from_record()` read-back.'
findings.suggested_fix: Add an explicit 'Depends on: e4df (must be merged first)' gate at the top of the plan's Scope section, and add a CI pre-check `python -c 'from rebar.signing import sign_opcert_manifest'` to the AC-7 lint/typecheck/test gate so the dependency is mechanically enforced.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in the plan whether `signing.sign_manifest` is globally repointed (enumifying all affected callers) or whether `_mcp_writes.py` and `_lib_writes.py::sign_manifest` are explicitly repointed to `sign_opcert_manifest` as separate edit targets.
findings.finding: The MCP seam repointing is ambiguous: the plan's 'MCP seam' paragraph says 'the MCP `sign_manifest` tool rides the same repointed path (it calls `signing.sign_manifest`'s successor seam)' — but the 'What' section only explicitly repoints `sign_plan_review()` and `transition_close.py` to call `sign_opcert_manifest`. `_mcp_writes.py::sign_manifest` calls `rebar.sign_manifest()` → `_lib_writes.sign_manifest()` → `signing.sign_manifest()` (the HMAC path). For the MCP claim to hold, either `signing.sign_manifest` itself must be globally repointed to Ed25519 (which would affect all HMAC callers including the `rebar sign` CLI, `attest.py`'s resign-probe path, etc., none of which are enumerated), or `_mcp_writes.py` must be explicitly repointed to call `sign_opcert_manifest` (and the file added to the edit set).
findings.location: What section / MCP seam paragraph; Scope section

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `refresh_attestation` (attest.py:538) to the plan's scope as a third plan-review signing call site that must also be repointed to `sign_opcert_manifest`, OR explicitly justify leaving it on the HMAC path for this story (e.g. defer to the HMAC-removal story).
findings.finding: `refresh_attestation` in `attest.py` (line 538) calls `signing.sign_manifest` directly — a third plan-review signing path not listed in scope. During the expand phase it issues a legacy HMAC cert while `sign_plan_review` issues an op-cert; since both target kind `plan-review`, the HMAC cert overwrites the op-cert in the last-writer-wins attestations map, silently downgrading certified attestations after every drift-refresh.
findings.location: Scope / What — Plan-review producer section; src/rebar/llm/plan_review/attest.py:538
findings.suggested_fix: Either add `refresh_attestation` to the 'In' scope list and AC-2 test it, or add a scope-out note explaining it is intentionally deferred (and note that drift-refresh will produce HMAC certs during the expand window, which is acceptable).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify that `signing.sign_opcert_manifest()` and `opcert.opcert_from_record()` from the 'storage keystone (e4df, change 660)' prerequisite are actually landed before this story begins, or explicitly re-scope this story to include their implementation if e4df is this story.
findings.finding: The plan's Why section asserts that the 'storage keystone (e4df, change 660) already gives `signing.sign_opcert_manifest()` an envelope-bearing SIGNATURE event and `opcert.opcert_from_record()` read-back' — but neither function nor any `opcert` module exists anywhere in the current codebase. If e4df/change 660 is a prerequisite story that must land first, this story's starting-state assumption is unverified and the plan may silently inherit unbuilt scope.
findings.location: Why section — 'The storage keystone (e4df, change 660) already gives `signing.sign_opcert_manifest()` an envelope-bearing `SIGNATURE` event and `opcert.opcert_from_record()` read-back.'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a hard dependency note: 'Blocked on e4df / change 660 landing' if they are a separate prerequisite, or if this story IS e4df, remove the 'already gives' framing and describe both functions as in-scope deliverables in the What section and Acceptance Criteria.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm that e4df (`op-cert storage + producer primitive`) is `closed` before this story begins, OR explicitly fold `sign_opcert_manifest` / `opcert.opcert_from_record` into this story's scope — the plan must not assume a seam that does not yet exist.
findings.finding: `signing.sign_opcert_manifest()` and `opcert.opcert_from_record()` — which every production-code AC in this story calls — do not exist in the codebase; prerequisite ticket e4df is still `in_progress` and `src/rebar/attest/opcert.py` is absent.
findings.location: Why section: 'The storage keystone (e4df, change 660) already gives `signing.sign_opcert_manifest()` an envelope-bearing `SIGNATURE` event and `opcert.opcert_from_record()` read-back'; What section: plan-review and close-gate producers call `signing.sign_opcert_manifest(...)`
findings.suggested_fix: Add an explicit prerequisite block at the top of the plan: 'Prerequisite: e4df must be in `closed` status before this story starts — verify that `signing.sign_opcert_manifest` is importable and `src/rebar/attest/opcert.py` exists.' Alternatively, if this story is meant to implement those primitives, move the e4df ACs into the Acceptance Criteria here and remove the 'already gives' framing.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an acceptance criterion that exercises the MCP `sign_manifest` tool path end-to-end (e.g. invoke the MCP tool against a test fixture and assert the resulting SIGNATURE event carries a DSSE envelope, not an HMAC hex field), or explicitly state that the MCP path inherits the change automatically with a rationale (e.g. 'MCP tool calls `signing.sign_manifest` which is renamed/delegated to `sign_opcert_manifest`; no separate AC needed because the function is the same object').
findings.finding: The MCP `sign_manifest` seam is listed in Scope (In) and described in the What section, but there is no acceptance criterion that verifies the MCP tool path actually emits an envelope-bearing SIGNATURE event after the cutover.
findings.location: Scope (In) — 'MCP `sign_manifest` seam'; What — MCP seam bullet; Acceptance Criteria — no corresponding AC

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in the plan whether `sign_opcert_manifest` populates a `head_sha` field on the reduced SIGNATURE record (so `compute_validity`'s unscoped fallback works), or whether `compute_validity` must be updated to extract the equivalent from `merged_log_commit` in the op-cert case.
findings.finding: `compute_validity`'s unscoped freshness fallback (plan-review branch, lines 666–676) reads `attestation.get('head_sha')` from the verify-result dict. If op-cert verify results don't populate `head_sha` (op-cert SIGNATURE events carry the SHA inside the DSSE payload as `merged_log_commit`, not as a top-level record field), the unscoped path always reads `head_sha=None`, causing every unscoped op-cert attestation to evaluate as `stale-head` regardless of actual HEAD.
findings.location: What — Read path (expand) section; src/rebar/llm/plan_review/attest.py:666-676
findings.suggested_fix: Populate `head_sha` on the op-cert SIGNATURE record (mirroring the HMAC record) so `compute_validity` requires no change, OR add an explicit note that `compute_validity` is updated in this story to read `merged_log_commit` from the op-cert record for the unscoped fallback.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the concrete mechanism by which the MCP `sign_manifest` tool is repointed: either update `_mcp_writes.py:245` to call `rebar.sign_opcert_manifest`, or clarify that `signing.sign_manifest` is being redirected to call `sign_opcert_manifest` internally (in which case document that the HMAC path is removed from `sign_manifest` in this story, not the contract story).
findings.finding: The MCP `sign_manifest` tool calls `rebar.sign_manifest → signing.sign_manifest` (HMAC). The plan says it 'rides the same repointed path' but names no concrete mechanism for repointing it — the function it calls (`signing.sign_manifest`) remains the HMAC implementation after this story.
findings.location: What — MCP seam section; src/rebar/_mcp_writes.py:245

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the MCP seam integration concretely: state whether `signing.sign_manifest` is renamed, wrapped, or replaced, and whether the MCP tool's call site requires an explicit edit, so an executor has a single unambiguous implementation path.
findings.finding: The What section describes the MCP seam as calling '`signing.sign_manifest`'s successor seam' without specifying whether this means (a) `sign_manifest` is renamed to `sign_opcert_manifest` in-place, (b) `sign_manifest` becomes a wrapper delegating to `sign_opcert_manifest`, or (c) the MCP tool's call site is updated — leaving the executor to choose the API shape.
findings.location: What section — MCP seam bullet

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly address the key-loss / key-rotation scenario: describe what outcome same-environment verify produces when `.opcert-key` is absent after attestations exist, and state whether re-signing or re-running the producing gate is the intended recovery path.
findings.finding: Key genesis creates a passphrase-free Ed25519 private key at `<tracker>/.opcert-key`; the plan does not describe a rollback or recovery path if this key file is lost, corrupted, or rotated — all prior envelope attestations would become permanently unverifiable by the same-environment verify path.
findings.location: What section, 'Environment key genesis' paragraph; AC-1; no rollback/recovery section exists.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace the `<material>` placeholder in the plan-review producer description with the exact expression (e.g. 'the SHA-256 hex of the plan file as computed by `sign_plan_review`'s existing manifest assembly') so an executor can implement it without investigation.
findings.finding: The plan-review producer description uses the angle-bracket placeholder `material_fingerprint=<material>` without defining where the material fingerprint value comes from in the plan-review flow — an executing agent must guess or investigate the source.
findings.location: What section — Plan-review producer bullet: `material_fingerprint=<material>`
findings.suggested_fix: State explicitly: `material_fingerprint=<hex SHA-256 of the plan file, already computed by the existing manifest assembly in sign_plan_review()>` or name the variable/attribute from the existing codebase.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify atomic key genesis (write private key to a temp path then rename, use O_EXCL / existence-check-before-write, or serialize genesis with a lock) and document what happens if the key files are found inconsistent on subsequent calls.
findings.finding: Key genesis lacks an atomic-write / idempotency guarantee: if two processes race to the check-then-create step, or if the writer is killed mid-write, the private key and public key files can become mismatched or corrupt with no recovery path specified.
findings.location: What — 'Environment key genesis' paragraph; AC item 1
findings.suggested_fix: Write the keypair to `<tracker>/.opcert-key.tmp` + `<tracker>/.opcert-key.pub.tmp`, verify the pair is consistent, then `os.rename()` (atomic on POSIX) to the final paths; on genesis entry, if `.opcert-key` already exists skip generation entirely (idempotent).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify an atomic key-genesis protocol (e.g., generate to a temp file then `os.replace`, or a `.opcert-key.lock` advisory lock) so concurrent first-use calls are safe and idempotent.
findings.finding: Key genesis has an unspecified concurrency story: if two signing calls race in the same tracker directory (e.g., parallel test workers or concurrent MCP tool invocations), both may attempt `ssh-keygen` simultaneously and the second write can clobber the first key, leaving the public key and private key out of sync.
findings.location: What / Environment key genesis; AC item 1
findings.suggested_fix: Write private key to `<tracker>/.opcert-key.tmp`, then `os.replace('.opcert-key.tmp', '.opcert-key')` (atomic on POSIX); if `.opcert-key` already exists at replace time, discard the temp file and reuse the existing key. This makes genesis idempotent under any number of concurrent callers.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify in the key genesis section how atomicity is achieved for the Ed25519 keypair (e.g. generate to a temp path, then `os.link` or `os.rename` only when both files are complete), mirroring the mkstemp+link pattern already used for the HMAC key.
findings.finding: The plan says key genesis 'mirrors the HMAC `.signing-key` genesis' but `ssh-keygen` writes both private and public key files non-atomically to their target paths. The existing HMAC genesis uses `mkstemp` + `os.link` (atomic exclusive create) to prevent a torn-key race. The plan does not specify the atomic strategy for the Ed25519 keypair genesis, leaving open a race where a concurrent first-use reads a half-written (zero-byte) private key and fails permanently.
findings.location: What — Environment key genesis section; AC-1
findings.suggested_fix: Generate to `.opcert-key.tmp` / `.opcert-key.pub.tmp` in the same directory, verify both are complete, then rename/link both atomically (link the private key last, since the public key without the private key is harmless).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an acceptance criterion that verifies the MCP `sign_manifest` seam: a manifest signed via the MCP tool produces an envelope-bearing `SIGNATURE` event (no HMAC hex), confirming the repointed path is exercised end-to-end.
findings.finding: The MCP `sign_manifest` seam is listed in Scope as "In" and described in the What section, but no acceptance criterion covers or verifies it. This is an uncovered deliverable.
findings.location: Scope section ('In: … MCP `sign_manifest` seam …') and What section ('MCP seam' paragraph); AC section has no corresponding criterion.
findings.suggested_fix: Add AC: "The MCP `sign_manifest` tool invokes `sign_opcert_manifest` (not the legacy HMAC path); a test asserts that a manifest signed via the MCP seam produces an envelope-bearing `SIGNATURE` event with the correct kind and DSSE principal."

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an acceptance criterion (or expand AC-4) to verify that an envelope attestation produced with a different environment's key surfaces the `foreign_key`-style non-verified outcome rather than an error.
findings.finding: The What section describes foreign-environment certificate verification behavior ('a cert from another environment surfaces the existing `foreign_key`-style non-verified outcome'), but no acceptance criterion verifies this behavior.
findings.location: What section, 'Read path (expand)' paragraph; AC section has no corresponding criterion.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extend the 'Degrade, never wedge' guarantee (and an AC-level test) to cover key-load failures (unreadable file, invalid key material), recording `{signed:false, error:…}` rather than propagating the exception.
findings.finding: The 'Degrade, never wedge' guarantee is specified and tested only for `ssh-keygen` unavailability; the plan does not address what happens when the key file exists but is unreadable (e.g., mode 0000 after a permissions change) or contains invalid key material — leaving an untested error path that could raise an unhandled exception during signing.
findings.location: What / Degrade, never wedge; AC item 6
findings.suggested_fix: Wrap the key-load call in a `try/except (OSError, ValueError, cryptography.exceptions.*)` block that falls through to the same `{signed:false, error:str(e)}` outcome already used for ssh-keygen absence.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (or extend AC2/AC4) that explicitly tests cross-environment key mismatch: a cert signed with key-A is presented to an environment holding key-B and the test asserts the result is a non-verified (not certified) outcome, not an error or silent pass.
findings.finding: The expand-phase read path introduces a new, security-relevant behavior — a cert from a foreign environment produces a 'non-verified' outcome — but no Acceptance Criterion includes a test that asserts this cross-environment key-mismatch path explicitly.
findings.location: What section ('a cert from another environment surfaces the existing foreign_key-style non-verified outcome') and Acceptance Criteria (none of AC1–AC6 cover this path)
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC2 or as AC2b: 'a test asserts that verify_signature called with a cert signed by a different environment key returns a non-verified / foreign_key outcome, not a certified one, and does not raise an exception.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/resign.py` in the plan's Scope/edit-targets (it calls `attest.sign_plan_review()` and will pick up the Ed25519 path automatically); update or note the stale 'local HMAC sign' docstring as part of this story's changes.
findings.finding: `resign_plan_review()` in `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/resign.py` calls `attest.sign_plan_review()` (line 126) and is an out-of-dir consumer of the changed symbol, but is not listed in the plan's edit targets or Scope section. When `sign_plan_review()` is repointed to emit Ed25519 op-certs, `resign_plan_review` will automatically use the new path — but its docstring ('a local HMAC sign') and the returned `key_id` field shape may diverge from reality. The plan should explicitly enumerate this file.
findings.location: What section / Plan-review producer; Scope section
findings.suggested_fix: Add `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/resign.py` to the Scope → In list with note 'docstring update only (sign_plan_review repoint is automatic)'; add a bullet under 'What → Plan-review producer' confirming resign.py is intentionally unmodified beyond the transitive repoint.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a structured log/warning (at minimum) on each signing failure or genesis error, distinct from the in-band record, so operators can aggregate and alert on signing-degraded runs.
findings.finding: Key genesis and signing failures have no structured observability instrumentation beyond the in-band {signed: false, error: …} record; operators have no metric/log/trace to detect silent degradation at scale.
findings.location: What — 'Degrade, never wedge' paragraph

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a task to this story's scope to write or update an ADR / CLAUDE.md section capturing the uniform-producer-signing decision, the expand/contract migration plan, the one-signature-per-verdict invariant, and the cross-environment verification policy.
findings.finding: The novel architectural decision — uniform Ed25519 producer signing replacing per-clone HMAC, the expand/contract two-phase migration, and the 'one signature per verdict, no double-signing' invariant — is not explicitly captured in an ADR, CLAUDE.md, or design-doc update within this story's scope.
findings.location: Why section; Scope section

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define behavior for partial/corrupt key state: specify whether genesis re-runs (regenerates both files), raises a clear user-facing error, or falls back to the in-band `{signed:false}` outcome — and add a test asserting the chosen path.
findings.finding: The lifecycle spec omits partial and corrupt key-state recovery: what happens when `.opcert-key` exists but `.opcert-key.pub` is absent (or vice versa), or when either file is present but contains invalid/truncated Ed25519 material?
findings.location: What / Environment key genesis; Scope / In: key genesis

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm (and specify in the plan) that `sign_opcert_manifest()` in `signing.py` delegates signing to `attest.sshsig.sign()` and envelope construction to `attest.dsse` — not re-implementing the SSHSIG subprocess path that already exists in `attest/authorship.py`.
findings.finding: The proposed `signing.sign_opcert_manifest()` function would combine (a) opcert-specific payload construction and (b) key-path + DSSE-PAE + sshsig.sign() mechanics — the latter is structurally identical to `attest.authorship.sign_authorship()`. The plan does not specify that `sign_opcert_manifest` will compose the existing `attest.sshsig.sign()` + `attest.dsse` primitives rather than re-implementing the SSHSIG-over-PAE loop itself. If the implementer copies the sign/verify pattern from `authorship.py` into `signing.py` without delegating to the substrate, that duplicates security-critical code in exactly the layer ADR 0044 was built to prevent.
findings.location: What section — 'Environment key genesis' and 'Plan-review producer' / 'Completion-verifier producer' paragraphs; also AC lines 1–3
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the 'Environment key genesis' paragraph: 'The signing primitive delegates to `attest.sshsig.sign(pae_bytes, key_path, namespace)` (availability-checked via `sshsig.ensure_available()`) and wraps the result in a `dsse.Envelope` via `attest.dsse` — no separate SSHSIG subprocess logic in `signing.py`.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a rollback-state assertion to the plan: document that after a rollback, tickets with envelope-bearing SIGNATURE events will read as `mismatch` under old code (not `certified`), and specify the compensating-forward recovery path (e.g., re-sign via the legacy HMAC path after rollback, or accept the gate-block as the documented tradeoff — the plan must state the choice explicitly).
findings.finding: The plan does not assert the schema STATE AFTER rollback for tickets that were already signed under the new DSSE envelope format. Once envelope-bearing SIGNATURE events are committed and pushed to the append-only git-backed event log, a code rollback leaves those records in place; the legacy verify_record code will run hmac.compare_digest against an envelope JSON string and emit 'mismatch', causing previously-certified tickets to appear uncertified. The plan says 'HMAC removal is the contract story' and implies rollback is safe, but never states what happens to already-written envelope records.
findings.location: What section → 'Read path (expand)' / Scope section → 'Out: HMAC removal (the contract story, 8f1d)'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
